essential_question: Can you find a circle's parts hiding in the real world, and then construct and label every one of them yourself with a compass?
objective: Measure the radius and diameter of real circular objects and identify a real sector and chord, then construct a circle with a compass and correctly label its center, radius, diameter, chord, arc, sector, and tangent.

agenda.title: Brief — From Vocabulary to Real Circles

content.heading: From naming parts to finding and building them
bullets: Yesterday: named every part of a circle and learned d = 2r.
Today: hunt three real circles in your world and measure them, then draw and label your own circle with a compass.
The test: can you measure a real radius and diameter accurately — and construct and correctly label all seven parts on one circle you draw yourself?

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Open the project card. Gather your compass, ruler, protractor, and paper. Decide which three real circles you'll hunt (a plate, a clock face, a wheel, a round table) and which real object will stand in for a sector and which for a chord before you start measuring.

content.heading: Measure the world, then construct to spec
bullets: Circle Hunt — find and measure the radius and diameter of three real circles; check that each pair satisfies d = 2r.
Identify one real sector and one real chord (not a diameter) and explain why each fits its definition.
Compass Build — draw one circle, mark its center, and draw and label a radius, a diameter, a chord, an arc, a sector, and a tangent.
Check your tangent with a protractor — it should meet its radius at close to a right angle.

content.heading: Get the numbers and labels down

content.preview: Session 3: defend why the diameter is always the longest chord and why a circle's radii are always congruent, then the checkpoint.

success_criteria.text: I can measure the radius and diameter of a real circular object and confirm d = 2r
I can construct a circle with a compass and correctly label its center, radius, diameter, chord, arc, sector, and tangent
I can identify a sector and a chord in the real world and check a tangent's right angle with a protractor

questions.prompt: List the three real circles you hunted (what each object was) and your measured radius and diameter for each, to the nearest 0.5 cm or 1/4 inch. Confirm each pair satisfies d = 2r.
questions.answer: Any three honest, plausible real objects with measured radius and diameter, e.g. a dinner plate r ≈ 11 cm / d ≈ 22 cm, a coin r ≈ 1.2 cm / d ≈ 2.4 cm, a clock face r ≈ 15 cm / d ≈ 30 cm.
questions.explain: Rubric: three named real objects with both measurements recorded (1 pt) + all three pairs correctly satisfying d = 2r within measurement tolerance (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Name one real-world example of a sector and one real-world example of a chord that is NOT a diameter, and explain why each fits that part's definition.
questions.answer: Sector: a slice of pizza or pie — bounded by two straight cuts from near the center and the crust's arc between them. Chord (not a diameter): a cable strung between two points on a circular fountain or arch that doesn't pass through the center.
questions.explain: Full credit: a plausible real sector (a wedge bounded by two radius-like edges and an arc) and a plausible real chord that clearly does not pass through the center.

questions.prompt: Use a compass to draw a circle. Mark the center C, then draw and label a radius, a diameter, a chord that is not the diameter, an arc, a sector, and a tangent line.
questions.answer: One compass-drawn circle with center C marked, a labeled radius, a labeled diameter through C (length = 2 × radius), a labeled chord not through C, a marked minor arc, a shaded sector between two radii, and a tangent line touching the circle at exactly one point.
questions.explain: Rubric: center, radius, and diameter correctly drawn and labeled (1 pt) + chord, arc, sector, and tangent all present and correctly labeled (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Reflect: which was harder to get exactly right — measuring a real circle's radius and diameter, or constructing and labeling every part on your own compass-drawn circle? What told you when your tangent line was actually correct?
